danger_signals.visual_form: colored bullets/energy bolts contrasting with background
distinct mechanical/organic enemy sprites entering screen

safety_signals.visual_form: empty playfield areas with no enemies or projectiles
collectible objects with distinct visual markers

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: morphing/transformation mechanic distinguishes player from static enemies
mechanical/robotic design language differentiation from player entity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: character model shifts form/appearance via morphing animation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: altered movement speed, attack patterns, or collision properties

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: increased enemy sprite density on screen; denser projectile patterns
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemies attack more frequently; projectile velocity increases

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: scrolling playfield reveals new enemy formations and environmental layout
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: boss or major threat appears at predetermined screen positions

environmental_storytelling.example: mechanical enemies suggest industrial/technological setting; visual complexity increases with progression
scrolling arena design communicates level geography and implied world structure

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: responds to input; navigates playfield; fires weapons
altered movement/attack parameters based on transformation state
enter screen and move in predictable patterns; minimal aggressive action
track player position; fire projectiles with targeting logic; execute evasion patterns

notes: Metal Morph (1994, SNES) is a shoot-em-up with core mechanic centered on player character morphing/transformation. Visual communication relies primarily on: (1) sprite-based distinction between player and enemies; (2) projectile contrast for threat signaling; (3) screen-space density for combat intensity feedback. World-state manifests through playfield progression (scrolling arena) and enemy formation complexity. Limited faction differentiation—bipolar player vs. enemy-force structure typical of shmup genre. Political/narrative content appears minimal in shmup context; focus is mechanical/tactical communication. TCRF source provided no substantial extraction data beyond general wiki framework.
